classification.lineage_note: H00526 Seth (gen 5) → H00264 Rev. John (gen 4) → H00098 John No.2 (gen 3) → H00030 John (gen 2) → H00007 Samuel (gen 1) → H00001 Thomas

compiler_connection.notes: On the compiler's paternal (Hooker surname) line. Marriage to Abigail Gay explains the middle name 'Gay' in the compiler's father Michael Gay Hooker. The Gay family farm at Hinsdale NH became the Hooker place.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: Seth(H00526) → [descendants to trace] → Michael Gay Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker

open_questions.question: Father Rev. John Hooker of Northampton — EH entry number
open_questions.notes: Son of Rev. John and Sarah (Worthington) Hooker of Northampton, MA. Rev. John descends through H00098 (John No. 2, b. 1695). Need EH entry number.
open_questions.action: Look up Rev. John Hooker of Northampton in EH index

military_service.notes: EH: 'though very young he had some service' — born 1759, so approximately age 15-18 during service

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Came to the Hinsdale homestead in 1789 through his marriage to Abigail Gay; worked the farm and made hats there for fifty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage carried Hookers north to Hinsdale, 1789
narrative_blocks.body: Born and raised at Northampton, he served briefly in the Revolution as a teenager and learned hatmaking. His 1789 marriage to Abigail Gay of Hinsdale brought him onto her father Bunker's homestead, establishing the Hooker family on what became their New Hampshire seat for four generations.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on her father Rev. Bunker Gay's homestead; her 1789 marriage to Seth Hooker brought him onto the farm.

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.
Great-grandson Charles bore Gay middle name in his honor
Two-greats-grandson Robert continued Gay middle name
Four-greats-grandson Michael carried Gay name into 20th century

documents.notes: Published 1792; first-person captivity narrative dictated to him by his Hinsdale neighbor Jemima Howe Tute.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Harvard College 1760 before settling at Hinsdale.
First settled pastor and effective founder of the Congregational church organized 1763.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Settled the Hinsdale homestead in 1763; the farm passed to his daughter Abigail when she married Seth Hooker in 1789.

career.notes: 47-year pastorate. Original parish embraced all of Hinsdale plus Vernon (later separated when Vermont became a state). Continued as nominal pastor and officiated weddings and funerals into old age.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter brought the Hooker family to Hinsdale
narrative_blocks.body: Five of his six children with Abigail Prentice died young; only daughter Abigail survived to marriage. Her 1789 wedding to Seth Hooker brought Seth onto the Gay homestead, beginning the four-generation Hooker tenure on what locals called the Hooker place.

narrative_blocks.header: His Gay middle name passed down five generations
narrative_blocks.body: Through his daughter Abigail's marriage to Seth Hooker, the Gay name entered the Hooker line as a middle name. Great-grandson Charles Gay, great-great-grandson Robert Gay, and four-greats-grandson Michael Gay all carried it forward into the twentieth century.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-seven years as Hinsdale's first pastor
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained at the founding of the First Congregational Church of Hinsdale in 1763, he served the same pulpit through the Revolution and into old age. His parish originally embraced all of Hinsdale plus the Vermont side at Vernon, until Vermont's statehood split the territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Published the Jemima Howe captivity narrative, 1792
narrative_blocks.body: His Hinsdale neighbor Jemima Howe had been carried off to Quebec by Abenaki raiders in 1755 and spent years in French captivity before her ransom. He recorded her oral testimony and published it as 'A Genuine and Correct Account of the Captivity, Sufferings & Deliverance of Mrs. Jemima Howe.'

narrative_blocks.header: Dedham roots in the 1636 founding wave
narrative_blocks.body: His great-grandfather John Gay was an original proprietor of Dedham, Massachusetts in 1636, the same year Thomas Hooker founded Hartford. Two Great Migration foundings, joined a century and a half later when Abigail Gay married Seth Hooker.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Raised her six children on the Gay homestead at Hinsdale; buried beside her husband on the property's cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried five of six children in fifteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Between 1773 and 1788 she lost five of her six children — Bunker Jr. at eleven, Calvin at six, John at thirteen, Henry at twenty-six, and Sally as an infant — to a series of family tragedies in frontier Hinsdale. Daughter Abigail alone survived, marrying Seth Hooker in 1789 and carrying the line forward.
